text: The surgeon identifies the ureter and mobilizes the colon from the lateral abdominal wall along the line of the wall, using surgical instruments to carefully separate the colon from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the colon, further identifying and mobilizing the ureter from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses the ureter as a landmark to trace the hilum of the left-sided non-functioning moiety in the kidney.
The surgeon meticulously dissects and mobilizes the colon near the hilum of the left-sided non-functioning moiety, reaching the upper pole of the non-functioning kidney.
The surgeon dissects the lower pole of the non-functioning moiety, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon clips the aberrant vessel located at the lower pole of the non-functioning moiety, securing it to prevent bleeding.
The video shows the lower pole artery, ureter, and the lower pole of the kidney, all clearly demarcated. These anatomical structures are visible as the surgeon continues the dissection.
The surgeon performs further medial dissection at the hilum of the left-sided non-functioning moiety, identifying and looping the left renal vein.
The surgeon dissects the renal artery located behind the renal vein and clips the artery, demonstrating the procedure visually.
After controlling the vessels, the surgeon performs further dissection to free the upper pole of the kidney, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the upper pole of the kidney, revealing the psoas muscle posteriorly.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the kidney, ensuring it is freed from surrounding tissues and structures.
The surgeon dismembers the non-functioning kidney from the normal kidney, separating the two structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon opens the collecting system of the normal kidney, revealing the internal structures of the renal pelvis and calyces.
The surgeon continues the dissection beyond the isthmus, further freeing the kidney from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon places a stay suture at the corner of the incision, securing the tissue in position.
The surgeon applies traction to facilitate the continued dismembering of the non-functioning kidney moiety from the normal kidney.
The surgeon completes the dismemberment of the non-functional kidney moiety, separating it entirely from the normal kidney.
The surgeon cuts the ureter and performs a pyelography on the normal kidney, visualizing the collecting system.
The surgeon places the excised specimen into a surgical retrieval bag for removal from the operative field.
